nodes.name: installer.binary.replace.atomic
nodes.summary:
When installing or upgrading a binary at /usr/lib/globular/bin/, the write
must use a temp file in the same directory followed by an atomic rename.
Direct overwrite of a running binary causes SIGBUS or text-file-busy errors
and leaves a partially-written binary on failure.

nodes.name: fm.purge.on.soft.uninstall
nodes.summary:
If the uninstall command deletes /var/lib/globular unconditionally (without
checking --purge), all cluster state is destroyed: PKI keys, etcd data
references, ScyllaDB config, service configs, and the install manifest.
This is unrecoverable without a full re-bootstrap from scratch.

nodes.name: installer.check.before.apply
nodes.summary:
Every installer step implements the Check/Apply pattern. Check() returns
whether the desired state is already in place. If Check passes, Apply is
skipped. This makes the installer idempotent: running it twice on a
correctly installed system is a no-op.
An AI agent must not add Apply logic that bypasses Check, and must not
weaken Check to always return false (which forces redundant re-application).

nodes.name: fm.stale.unit.on.start
nodes.summary:
When a unit file is written to /etc/systemd/system/ and systemctl start
is called without an intervening daemon-reload, systemd uses the previously
loaded definition. The new unit file is on disk but ignored until the next
daemon-reload. The service may start on the wrong port, with wrong
environment variables, or with outdated ExecStart arguments.

nodes.name: metric_warning:etcd_fsync_latency_ms
nodes.summary: High etcd fsync latency risks leader election instability and workflow dispatch timeouts

metadata.explanation: High etcd fsync latency risks leader election instability and workflow dispatch timeouts

nodes.name: installer.purge.explicit.flag.only
nodes.summary:
The uninstall command has two modes: soft uninstall (removes binaries and units,
preserves /var/lib/globular) and purge (deletes all state). Purge must only
execute when --purge is explicitly set. Running uninstall without --purge must
never delete cluster state, PKI, or the manifest.
An AI agent must not add logic that purges state unconditionally on uninstall.

nodes.name: installer.dir.permissions.traversable
nodes.summary:
The globular user and the root user are not the only principals that need
to traverse /var/lib/globular. The TLS cert paths and socket paths must be
reachable by service processes running under different users. Directories
set to 0750 break cert loading for non-globular service accounts.
This invariant is enforced in filesystem.go and tested by install-day0.sh.

nodes.name: metric_warning:etcd_disk_percent
nodes.summary: etcd disk full triggers NOSPACE alarm, blocks all writes, halts convergence

metadata.explanation: etcd disk full triggers NOSPACE alarm, blocks all writes, halts convergence

nodes.name: installer.root.required
nodes.summary:
The installer writes to /usr/lib/, /var/lib/, /etc/systemd/system/, and
/etc/hosts — all requiring root. RequireRootStep must run as the first
step in any mutating plan. There must be no code path that silently
continues with degraded behaviour when not running as root.

nodes.name: metric_warning:minio_offline_disks
nodes.summary: Offline MinIO disks risk artifact availability and violate topology contract

metadata.explanation: Offline MinIO disks risk artifact availability and violate topology contract

nodes.name: metric_warning:scylla_disk_percent
nodes.summary: ScyllaDB disk pressure risks workflow/AI memory backend availability

metadata.explanation: ScyllaDB disk pressure risks workflow/AI memory backend availability

nodes.name: fm.partial.install.no.manifest
nodes.summary:
The install plan applies steps sequentially. If a step fails after earlier
steps have already written binaries, unit files, or directories, the manifest
is never written (it is only written on full success). On the next run, the
installer sees no manifest and re-applies all steps from scratch. This is
usually safe — but if the failed step left a partially-written file or a
half-configured service, the re-run may overwrite or conflict with it.

nodes.name: fm.binary.overwrite.sigbus
nodes.summary:
If a running service binary is overwritten in place (open + write) rather
than via temp-file rename, the running process's memory-mapped pages
become inconsistent with the on-disk file. This causes SIGBUS when the OS
tries to page in code from the new file content, or leaves the binary in a
partially-written state if the write is interrupted.

nodes.name: fm.fetch.tampered.binary
nodes.summary:
FetchFileStep downloads a file over HTTP/HTTPS and moves it into place
without verifying a SHA-256 or signature. A MITM attack, CDN cache poisoning,
or a simple URL redirect can cause a tampered or wrong-version binary to
be installed as a Globular service binary. The installer reports success.

nodes.name: installer.fetch.checksum.required
nodes.summary:
FetchFileStep downloads files over HTTP/HTTPS. Without a checksum check,
a man-in-the-middle attack, CDN corruption, or a redirect to a wrong URL
can install a tampered binary with no error. Any FetchFileStep that installs
an executable or config file must specify an expected SHA-256 and verify it
before moving the file into place.
Current gap: FetchFileStep has no signature verification (medium risk, candidate).

nodes.name: installer.manifest.written.after.full.success
nodes.summary:
/var/lib/globular/install-manifest.json is the record of a successful install.
It must be written only after every step in the plan has applied successfully.
Writing a partial manifest (written mid-plan or after a failed step) is
worse than no manifest: subsequent runs see it as complete and skip steps
that were never applied.

nodes.name: installer.daemon.reload.before.start
nodes.summary:
After writing or modifying a systemd unit file in /etc/systemd/system/,
daemon-reload must be called before starting or restarting the service.
Starting with a stale unit means systemd runs the old unit definition,
silently ignoring the new one.

nodes.name: fm.uid.collision
nodes.summary:
If the installer creates the globular user with a hardcoded UID (e.g., 999),
it collides with an existing system user on distributions that assign UIDs
differently. The user creation fails or worse, silently reuses the wrong
account. Files owned by the colliding UID gain unintended access to
globular service data.

nodes.name: installer.spec.validated.before.plan
nodes.summary:
SpecPlanBuilder accepts a parsed spec and constructs the step plan. If the
spec is structurally invalid (unknown step type, missing required param,
version field absent), the plan builder may silently skip the bad step or
panic mid-install. Validation must reject the spec before any steps are
constructed or executed.

nodes.name: installer.no.hardcoded.uid
nodes.summary:
UIDs and GIDs are assigned by the OS and vary between distributions and
installations. Hardcoding UID 999 for the globular user (or any other UID)
will collide with an existing system account on some machines.
Always use user.Lookup("globular") and group.Lookup("globular") to resolve.

nodes.name: fm.spec.stale.at.release
nodes.summary:
The installer embeds YAML specs from internal/specs/. These specs are
generated from the services package manifests via make sync-specs. If
check-specs is not run in CI before release, the embedded specs can be
stale: wrong binary paths, wrong port assignments, or missing new services.
The installer applies the stale spec without error; services start on the
wrong port or not at all.

nodes.name: metric_warning:workflow_failed_runs_15m
nodes.summary: Workflow failures indicate convergence risk and may threaten reconcile fairness

metadata.explanation: Workflow failures indicate convergence risk and may threaten reconcile fairness

invariants.title: The install manifest must be written only after all steps succeed
invariants.summary:
/var/lib/globular/install-manifest.json is the record of a successful install.
It must be written only after every step in the plan has applied successfully.
Writing a partial manifest (written mid-plan or after a failed step) is
worse than no manifest: subsequent runs see it as complete and skip steps
that were never applied.

invariants.title: All OS-mutating operations require root; no silent fallback to user-level
invariants.summary:
The installer writes to /usr/lib/, /var/lib/, /etc/systemd/system/, and
/etc/hosts — all requiring root. RequireRootStep must run as the first
step in any mutating plan. There must be no code path that silently
continues with degraded behaviour when not running as root.

invariants.title: /var/lib/globular directories must be world-traversable (0755), not 0750
invariants.summary:
The globular user and the root user are not the only principals that need
to traverse /var/lib/globular. The TLS cert paths and socket paths must be
reachable by service processes running under different users. Directories
set to 0750 break cert loading for non-globular service accounts.
This invariant is enforced in filesystem.go and tested by install-day0.sh.

invariants.title: Install specs must be validated before plan construction begins
invariants.summary:
SpecPlanBuilder accepts a parsed spec and constructs the step plan. If the
spec is structurally invalid (unknown step type, missing required param,
version field absent), the plan builder may silently skip the bad step or
panic mid-install. Validation must reject the spec before any steps are
constructed or executed.

invariants.title: Every step must Check before Apply; Apply is skipped if Check passes
invariants.summary:
Every installer step implements the Check/Apply pattern. Check() returns
whether the desired state is already in place. If Check passes, Apply is
skipped. This makes the installer idempotent: running it twice on a
correctly installed system is a no-op.
An AI agent must not add Apply logic that bypasses Check, and must not
weaken Check to always return false (which forces redundant re-application).

invariants.title: Binary files must be replaced atomically via temp-file rename
invariants.summary:
When installing or upgrading a binary at /usr/lib/globular/bin/, the write
must use a temp file in the same directory followed by an atomic rename.
Direct overwrite of a running binary causes SIGBUS or text-file-busy errors
and leaves a partially-written binary on failure.

invariants.title: Destructive purge (deleting /var/lib/globular) requires an explicit --purge flag
invariants.summary:
The uninstall command has two modes: soft uninstall (removes binaries and units,
preserves /var/lib/globular) and purge (deletes all state). Purge must only
execute when --purge is explicitly set. Running uninstall without --purge must
never delete cluster state, PKI, or the manifest.
An AI agent must not add logic that purges state unconditionally on uninstall.

invariants.title: systemctl daemon-reload must be called after every unit file write before start/restart
invariants.summary:
After writing or modifying a systemd unit file in /etc/systemd/system/,
daemon-reload must be called before starting or restarting the service.
Starting with a stale unit means systemd runs the old unit definition,
silently ignoring the new one.

invariants.title: Network-fetched files must be checksum-verified before use
invariants.summary:
FetchFileStep downloads files over HTTP/HTTPS. Without a checksum check,
a man-in-the-middle attack, CDN corruption, or a redirect to a wrong URL
can install a tampered binary with no error. Any FetchFileStep that installs
an executable or config file must specify an expected SHA-256 and verify it
before moving the file into place.
Current gap: FetchFileStep has no signature verification (medium risk, candidate).

invariants.title: Never hardcode a UID or GID; always resolve by name
invariants.summary:
UIDs and GIDs are assigned by the OS and vary between distributions and
installations. Hardcoding UID 999 for the globular user (or any other UID)
will collide with an existing system account on some machines.
Always use user.Lookup("globular") and group.Lookup("globular") to resolve.

failure_modes.title: Hardcoded UID for globular user collides with existing system account
failure_modes.summary:
If the installer creates the globular user with a hardcoded UID (e.g., 999),
it collides with an existing system user on distributions that assign UIDs
differently. The user creation fails or worse, silently reuses the wrong
account. Files owned by the colliding UID gain unintended access to
globular service data.
symptoms: adduser fails with UID already in use
globular user created but maps to a different username (avahi, nobody, etc.)
service data files readable by unexpected users
failure_modes.root_cause:
If the installer creates the globular user with a hardcoded UID (e.g., 999),
it collides with an existing system user on distributions that assign UIDs
differently. The user creation fails or worse, silently reuses the wrong
account. Files owned by the colliding UID gain unintended access to
globular service data.

failure_modes.title: Embedded specs out of sync with packaged services — make check-specs not run
failure_modes.summary:
The installer embeds YAML specs from internal/specs/. These specs are
generated from the services package manifests via make sync-specs. If
check-specs is not run in CI before release, the embedded specs can be
stale: wrong binary paths, wrong port assignments, or missing new services.
The installer applies the stale spec without error; services start on the
wrong port or not at all.
symptoms: installed services report wrong port in globular service discovery
new services added to packages/ not present in installer spec
make check-specs fails post-release with diff between expected and actual
failure_modes.root_cause:
The installer embeds YAML specs from internal/specs/. These specs are
generated from the services package manifests via make sync-specs. If
check-specs is not run in CI before release, the embedded specs can be
stale: wrong binary paths, wrong port assignments, or missing new services.
The installer applies the stale spec without error; services start on the
wrong port or not at all.

failure_modes.title: Plan fails mid-way; files written but manifest never persisted
failure_modes.summary:
The install plan applies steps sequentially. If a step fails after earlier
steps have already written binaries, unit files, or directories, the manifest
is never written (it is only written on full success). On the next run, the
installer sees no manifest and re-applies all steps from scratch. This is
usually safe — but if the failed step left a partially-written file or a
half-configured service, the re-run may overwrite or conflict with it.
symptoms: installer exits with error but /usr/lib/globular/bin/ contains some binaries
systemd units are present but service is not enabled or running
/var/lib/globular/install-manifest.json does not exist after a reported failure
second run re-applies all steps, some of which conflict with partial state
failure_modes.root_cause:
The install plan applies steps sequentially. If a step fails after earlier
steps have already written binaries, unit files, or directories, the manifest
is never written (it is only written on full success). On the next run, the
installer sees no manifest and re-applies all steps from scratch. This is
usually safe — but if the failed step left a partially-written file or a
half-configured service, the re-run may overwrite or conflict with it.

failure_modes.title: Binary replaced non-atomically while service is running — SIGBUS or corruption
failure_modes.summary:
If a running service binary is overwritten in place (open + write) rather
than via temp-file rename, the running process's memory-mapped pages
become inconsistent with the on-disk file. This causes SIGBUS when the OS
tries to page in code from the new file content, or leaves the binary in a
partially-written state if the write is interrupted.
symptoms: service crashes with SIGBUS immediately after an upgrade
binary at /usr/lib/globular/bin/ has file size = 0 or is truncated
journalctl shows text file busy error during install
failure_modes.root_cause:
If a running service binary is overwritten in place (open + write) rather
than via temp-file rename, the running process's memory-mapped pages
become inconsistent with the on-disk file. This causes SIGBUS when the OS
tries to page in code from the new file content, or leaves the binary in a
partially-written state if the write is interrupted.

failure_modes.title: Service started with old unit definition because daemon-reload was skipped
failure_modes.summary:
When a unit file is written to /etc/systemd/system/ and systemctl start
is called without an intervening daemon-reload, systemd uses the previously
loaded definition. The new unit file is on disk but ignored until the next
daemon-reload. The service may start on the wrong port, with wrong
environment variables, or with outdated ExecStart arguments.
symptoms: service starts successfully but uses wrong port or binary path
systemctl status shows unit definition differs from /etc/systemd/system/ file
changes to unit file have no effect until manual daemon-reload
failure_modes.root_cause:
When a unit file is written to /etc/systemd/system/ and systemctl start
is called without an intervening daemon-reload, systemd uses the previously
loaded definition. The new unit file is on disk but ignored until the next
daemon-reload. The service may start on the wrong port, with wrong
environment variables, or with outdated ExecStart arguments.

failure_modes.title: Network-fetched binary installed without checksum verification
failure_modes.summary:
FetchFileStep downloads a file over HTTP/HTTPS and moves it into place
without verifying a SHA-256 or signature. A MITM attack, CDN cache poisoning,
or a simple URL redirect can cause a tampered or wrong-version binary to
be installed as a Globular service binary. The installer reports success.
symptoms: service starts but behaves unexpectedly or crashes immediately
binary hash does not match the expected release artifact
no checksum error in installer output (verification was never done)
failure_modes.root_cause:
FetchFileStep downloads a file over HTTP/HTTPS and moves it into place
without verifying a SHA-256 or signature. A MITM attack, CDN cache poisoning,
or a simple URL redirect can cause a tampered or wrong-version binary to
be installed as a Globular service binary. The installer reports success.

failure_modes.title: /var/lib/globular deleted during uninstall without explicit --purge
failure_modes.summary:
If the uninstall command deletes /var/lib/globular unconditionally (without
checking --purge), all cluster state is destroyed: PKI keys, etcd data
references, ScyllaDB config, service configs, and the install manifest.
This is unrecoverable without a full re-bootstrap from scratch.
symptoms: /var/lib/globular is empty or missing after uninstall
PKI certs and private keys are gone
etcd cannot start (data directory missing)
re-install required to recover
failure_modes.root_cause:
If the uninstall command deletes /var/lib/globular unconditionally (without
checking --purge), all cluster state is destroyed: PKI keys, etcd data
references, ScyllaDB config, service configs, and the install manifest.
This is unrecoverable without a full re-bootstrap from scratch.
